summary: Full multi-agent code+docs review: defensive, resilient, tested, simulated — find, adversarially verify, plan fix waves

plan.headline: Subsystems are fundamentally sound: the 4 CRITICAL/HIGH "wedge-class" verifications (reorg-race, coins co-commit torn write, batch-write double-unlock, mutex-init race, compact-block UAF, wallet next_child loss) were all REFUTED on the code — the real confirmed work is a tight set of observability gaps (bare printf/fprintf on consensus-reject and heal paths), 2 dead-code/doc-staleness items, a real onion-address cross-thread data race (MEDIUM, bounded blast radius), and genuine test/sim DEPTH gaps (mid-repair atomicity + seed-driven crash-point fuzz). Land the mechanical wave fast under build+lint+test_parallel; the only RISKY item is the onion address-snapshot lock.

confirmed_critical_high.claim: bad-diffbits consensus-reject diagnostic (header/expected nBits, prev_height, prev_bits) goes to stdout via printf+fflush, not the structured event sink; additionally contextual_check_block_header emits NO EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK at all, unlike its sibling wrappers. Verified: this is the only printf in check_block.c, and the file already has consensus_reject_block_emit() at lines 38-54.
confirmed_critical_high.fix: Delete printf+fflush(stdout) at 313-317. Preferred (B): make contextual_check_block_header() emit on failure like check_block_header()/check_block() via consensus_reject_block_emit(), so ALL contextual-header rejects (bad-equihash-solution-size, bad-diffbits, time-too-old, bad-fork-at-checkpoint, bad-version) gain EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK uniformly; carry the nBits detail by appending to state->reject_reason. Minimal (A): replace printf with event_emitf(EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK,0,'hash=%s reason=bad-diffbits header=0x%08x expected=0x%08x prev_height=%d prev_bits=0x%08x',...).

confirmed_critical_high.claim: Two coupled real issues confirmed by grep. (1) 'missing-input: ...' diagnostic at connect_block.c:396 is a bare printf to stdout on the bad-txns-inputs-missingorspent consensus-reject path — same observability class as check_block.c:313. (2) struct validation_state.missing_txid/missing_vout/has_missing_utxo (validation.h:43-45) are SET at connect_block.c:400-402 and reset at validation.h:56-58 but have ZERO readers anywhere in lib/app/config (grep-confirmed); the line-389 comment 'Find the specific missing input for self-healing recovery' describes a flow that no longer exists (legacy connect/disconnect engine purged). connect_block's only production caller is the offline -reindex-chainstate tool (boot_index.c:244), where the fatal-on-failure behavior is correct; live missing-input recovery is the reducer prevout_unresolved path (script_validate_stage.c).
confirmed_critical_high.fix: (a) Replace the printf at 396 with event_emitf(EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK,0,'reason=bad-txns-inputs-missingorspent h=%d ...') routing through the sink. (b) Per less-is-more doctrine, DELETE the dead missing_txid/missing_vout/has_missing_utxo fields + their reset + the population block at 390-405, and fix the stale 'self-healing recovery' comment. Do NOT wire process_block_note_utxo_failure() into connect_block (it has zero production callers, is documented NOT-called from process_block, and would corrupt an in-progress reindex via tip stage-unwind on >=5 fails).

confirmed_critical_high.location: lib/net/src/onion_service.c:21-34 (struct onion_context g_onion_ctx.address[128]); write at onion_service.c:907-911 (onion_service_set_address); reads at :149,:721 (serve_status/serve_landing_page) and :904 (onion_service_get_address)
confirmed_critical_high.claim: CONFIRMED real C data race / UB (TSan-detectable). onion_service_set_address() runs on the spawned zcl_tor_monitor thread (tor_integration.c:237/270-271) and does an unsynchronized snprintf into g_onion_ctx.address[128]; the dynhost request handler is registered (tor_integration.c:308-311) BEFORE the monitor/tor threads spawn, so a request CAN land mid-write, and onion_service_get_address (returning the raw internal pointer at :904) is also read from health/store/net_controller threads. Blast radius is BOUNDED: fixed 128-byte member of a static zero-init struct (NOT the phashBlock realloc-UAF class; no OOB crash — torn read terminates within the zero-padded buffer), one-shot empty->valid transition near startup. Worst realistic outcome is a momentarily garbled/empty onion string in served HTML/status JSON. Hence MEDIUM, not HIGH.
confirmed_critical_high.fix: Add a static pthread_mutex_t g_onion_addr_mtx. Serialize the write (snprintf/clear at :911/:930) under it, and add a helper static size_t onion_copy_address(char *out,size_t cap) that locks, copies into a caller stack buffer, unlocks; convert serve_status(:721), serve_landing_page(:149), and onion_service_get_address(:904) to snapshot-copy under the lock (get_address ideally becomes bool onion_service_get_address(char *out,size_t cap), touching node_health_service.c:157, store_view.c, net_controller.c:94). Add a struct comment documenting the invariant + a -fsanitize=thread test that hammers get/serve while a writer flips set_address.

confirmed_critical_high.location: lib/test/src/test_parallel.c:21-56 (TEST_LIST/SPEC_LIST X-macros) vs lib/test/src/test.c:27-1095 (hand-written test_X()/spec_X() dispatch)
confirmed_critical_high.claim: CONFIRMED: the two test registries share NO source of truth and there is NO machine-checked gate (grep over tools/lint/, Makefile, test_make_lint_gates.c finds none). Registries are perfectly in sync TODAY (381 test + 28 spec both sides), so this is a PREVENTION gap, not a present bug. Failure mode is silent in BOTH directions: a test added to one runner but not the other links+runs clean while silently dropping coverage; since CLAUDE.md mandates test_parallel as THE runner, a missing X-macro entry drops the test from every normal make test.
confirmed_critical_high.fix: Add gate INSIDE the existing test_make_lint_gates.c source-grep harness (no new build wiring): t_test_registry_in_sync reads test_parallel.c, extracts the X(name) set in TEST_LIST and SPEC_LIST; reads test.c, extracts the test_<name>( and spec_<name>( call sets; ASSERTS set-equality both directions, printing offending names on mismatch. Pure source-text diff (no execution), matching the harness's existing grep-and-assert pattern. Do NOT regenerate test_parallel.c from test.c (larger/riskier; X-macro is the canonical form).

confirmed_critical_high.claim: CONFIRMED (HIGH->MEDIUM): verdict logic is correct on inspection but has real isolated-coverage gaps in the only consumer of it. (a) NO test ever feeds a DECREASING height, so the load-bearing reorg invariant (only a new HWM updates last_advance_ts; a height<hwm reorg must NOT reset the stall timer) is untested — the stall test feeds FLAT height, not a decrease. (b) RSS baseline latch boundary is '>=' (soak_harness.c:107) but the exact-boundary timing is never asserted, so a >=->> regression would not be caught. (c) verdict priority is only ever tested with ONE failure mode active, so CRASH-beats-STALL etc. is unproven. (Refuted sub-claims: crash-during-warmup and crash-sticky-after-resume ARE already covered.) MEDIUM because it is a CI test-coverage gap in an offline analyzer with no I/O; #6's real gate is the 168h live soak.
confirmed_critical_high.fix: Add 3 focused hermetic cases (matching the existing synthetic-stream pattern, reset-safe under test_parallel): (1) REORG INVARIANT — healthy window then a height DROP below tip_hwm while wall-clock advances past max_tip_stall_sec; assert NOT FAIL_TIP_STALL from the reorg alone (+ companion: a real flat stall after a reorg DOES trip STALL). (2) RSS BOUNDARY — min-RSS sample at exactly ts==warmup_sec latches (later climb trips FAIL_RSS_WALK) vs at ts==warmup_sec-60 does NOT latch; pins the >= boundary. (3) PRIORITY — one stream satisfying CRASH+TIP_STALL (and a second TOO_SHORT+RSS_WALK); assert higher-priority verdict wins. Note warmup units are SECONDS not ms.

fix_waves.theme: SAFE-MECHANICAL observability + dead-code + doc corrections (no behavior change to live consensus/forward path)
items: check_block.c:313 — replace bad-diffbits printf+fflush(stdout) with EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK emission; make contextual_check_block_header emit on failure like its sibling wrappers (closes the broader 'contextual-header rejects emit no event' gap).

connect_block.c:396 — replace 'missing-input' printf with structured event; DELETE the dead missing_txid/missing_vout/has_missing_utxo fields (validation.h:43-45,56-58 + connect_block.c:400-402) and fix the stale line-389 'self-healing recovery' comment to point at the reducer prevout_unresolved path.

coins_view_sqlite.c — add event_emitf on the two currently-uninstrumented HEAL verdicts (class=bounded-auto-rewind at ~:535, class=historical-coins-lag at ~:543) so every tip-check verdict reaches the event stream; optionally add a coins dump_state_json + zcl_state subsystem=coins.

onion_service.c:108-109 — close db on the get_self_stats open-failure path (mirror the already-correct :498-500 pattern) and add LOG_FAIL; the :498-500 'leak' was a false positive (close already correct), only the missing LOG_FAIL there is real.
onion_service.c:432-434 — replace fprintf-to-stderr on prepare failure in populate_directory_from_chain with LOG_FAIL/log_jsonf (service-path silent-error gate).
block_index_projection.c:142-150 — wrap create_schema DDL failures in EV_DB_SCHEMA_FAILED before the fprintf.
wallet_sqlite.c:813-816 / :855 — remove the unreachable 'return true' after LOG_FAIL (dead code; LOG_FAIL already returns false). Clarifies the write-failure contract.
wallet_sqlite.c read_sapling_seed:832-836 — add LOG_WARN before 'return false' on decrypt failure so a typo'd ZCL_WALLET_PASSPHRASE surfaces as an observable encryption mismatch.
sapling_keys.c:70-73,137-139,205-208 — add LOG_WARN on the MAX_SAPLING_KEYS keystore-full return so callers see why false was returned.
rpc_client.c:173-174 — check send() return values (partial/failed send) and LOG_ERR + close on error.

http_middleware.c:104-117 / mcp middleware.c:324-330 — log the backward-monotonic-clock event and snap *last_us=now (prevents silent rate-limit wedge on NTP/VM clock rewind); bounds-cap timeout_ms before deadline calc to avoid timespec overflow.
ldb_snapshot.c:95-109 — check unlink() result on the EEXIST hardlink-fallback path; fail with a clear message instead of silently retrying link() against a still-present file.
block_index_new / chainstate.c:675 — add 'if (!hash) return NULL;' null-guard (mirrors codebase pattern).
Doc fixes (see doc_fixes list).
fix_waves.risk: Each item adds logging / deletes provably-dead code / corrects a doc, with no change to consensus, reorg, or forward-progress behavior. Lowest possible blast radius.
fix_waves.gate: make -j$(nproc) && make lint && make test_parallel — read the 'N passed, M failed' line; lint must stay green (these touch error-logging gates, so they should INCREASE compliance). No live deploy.

fix_waves.theme: SAFE-MECHANICAL test + CI-gate additions (no production code change)
items: Add t_test_registry_in_sync to test_make_lint_gates.c: source-text set-equality of TEST_LIST/SPEC_LIST (test_parallel.c) vs test_X()/spec_X() dispatch (test.c), both directions.

Add 3 soak_harness edge cases: reorg-invariant (decreasing height must NOT reset stall timer), RSS-latch boundary (>= at exact warmup_sec), verdict-priority contention (CRASH>STALL, TOO_SHORT>RSS_WALK).

Add a fast hermetic kill-9 doc note OR confirm CI coverage: NOTE this was a false positive — make ci already runs kill9 via ci-mvp-gates (ZCL_STRESS_TESTS=1 injected by mvp_gate) + test-crash; only the doc comment in test_kill9_recovery.c:56-71 is misleading and should add the 'make ci DOES run this' sentence.

Add an Equihash internal-branch negative or two (out-of-order-indices at equihash.c:444, non-distinct at :451) — currently only hit incidentally by perturbation; the broad equihash coverage claim of 'no tests' was a false positive (coverage exists in test_chain.c/test_domain_consensus_equihash*.c).

Add MCP boundary-error-code assertions (ENUM_MISMATCH/STRING_TOO_LONG/INT_OUT_OF_RANGE) — NOTE test_mcp_fuzz.c already exists and is wired at test.c:1058; this is only an incremental strengthening, not the missing-suite the original finding claimed.
fix_waves.risk: Tests + a source-grep lint gate only; cannot regress production behavior. The CI-gate prevents a whole class of silent coverage loss.
fix_waves.gate: make test_parallel green + the new lint gate passes against current (in-sync) registries; new soak/equihash cases pass deterministically.

fix_waves.theme: RISKY — onion-address cross-thread snapshot lock (behavior-changing, needs adversarial proof)
items: onion_service.c — add g_onion_addr_mtx; serialize the set_address write and convert serve_status/serve_landing_page/onion_service_get_address to snapshot-copy under the lock. Consider changing onion_service_get_address to bool ...(char *out,size_t cap) and updating its 3-4 external callers (node_health_service.c:157, store_view.c, net_controller.c:94).

Add a dedicated -fsanitize=thread CI target / test that spawns a reader thread hammering get/serve_status while a writer flips set_address, to lock in TSan-cleanliness (this is the only confirmed real concurrency defect in the whole review).
fix_waves.risk: Touches the onion request-serving read path and an external API signature; lock-order and the get_address contract change must be reviewed. Bounded blast radius (non-consensus, optional -tor subsystem) but still behavior-changing.
fix_waves.gate: build+lint+test_parallel green AND the new TSan target reports zero races on the onion get/set path AND a manual -tor smoke (onion address still served correctly). No consensus/forward-path impact, so no copy-prove needed; standard review sign-off.

doc_fixes: check_block.h:127-162 (ContextualCheckBlock docstring) — state explicitly that Check 2 (finality) uses block header timestamp NOT MTP, matching zclassicd ConnectBlock; BIP113/LOCKTIME_MEDIAN_TIME_PAST applies ONLY to mempool acceptance, not block connection. Reference the code comment at check_block.c:413.
connect_block.c:1-36 header — correct the '14 checks matching zclassicd exactly' count to note the C23 additions (ZIP-209 turnstile, Sapling-root sanity, BIP30 self-write kill-9 recovery extension).

BOOT_INVARIANTS.md §3 — name the exact column(s) that store the height-stamped utxo_sha3 commitment + the CREATE statement + a worked recompute/compare example, and add forward links to the write site (snapshot-import) and read site (coins_reconcile_stale_anchor) so operators can navigate the stale-anchor self-heal.
utxo_recovery_restore.c:175-190 — expand the 'Defensive: datadir is interpolated into shell commands' comment to name the strcspn(datadir, "\"'$`|&;<>()") metacharacter-rejection on line 184.

utxo_apply_stage.c:469-474 FRESHNESS CONTRACT — clarify the snapshot-isolation semantics (projection_live_lookup at :491 sees all PRIOR-COMMITTED blocks plus coins added earlier in THIS block's apply; cross-block freshness holds because the prior block committed and released progress_store_tx_lock before this block's unwind/apply).
stage.c:311-314 — revise the atomicity comment to say all step writes (log rows + inverse-deltas + coins_kv + cursor advance) land in ONE COMMIT, not a lock-free single atomic cursor update.

test_kill9_recovery.c:56-71 Gating note — add the sentence that make ci DOES exercise this gate via ci-mvp-gates (ZCL_STRESS_TESTS=1 + ZCL_TEST_ONLY=kill9 injected by the mvp_gate macro); only bare make test skips it.
seed_tape.h:10-27 — document which of Phase 6a/6b/6c have shipped vs TODO, and link to test_seed_tape.c/test_postmortem.c + the recovery-simulator task (#31).
postmortem.c:1-50 — document the async-signal-safety contract (only signal_* / write / memcpy in the handler path; no malloc/fprintf) and the g_postmortem_in_handler re-entrancy guard.

onion_service.c module header — document the threading model: handle_request runs on Tor's dynhost worker thread; address is written once by the tor monitor thread; access only under g_onion_addr_mtx (after Wave 3).

Recovery-test surface index (MVP.md or FRAMEWORK.md) — list the recovery test set: test_kill9_recovery, test_boot_coins_anchor_dual_store_recovery, test_recovery_policy, test_utxo_apply_crash_replay, test_stage_reorg_unwind_parity, and the make test-crash full-binary harness, each linked to its MVP criterion.
sim_test_gaps: MID-REPAIR ATOMICITY (highest value): no test forces a crash BETWEEN cursor mutations inside the frontier-repair transaction. Add a test that seeds a broken progress.kv, begins the repair, force-closes/reopens progress_store between cursor writes (or asserts the repair runs in a single progress_store transaction so a crash is all-or-nothing), then re-runs reconcile_light and asserts idempotent convergence to the same golden cursor/frontier vector. The existing white-box repair tests only 'run once, check end state' and cannot catch a torn-repair partial write.

SEED-DRIVEN CRASH-POINT FUZZ on tip-sync recovery (task #31): no test consumes lib/sim/seed_tape to randomize the crash POINT. Add test_reducer_frontier_crash_recovery.c that uses seed_tape to (a) build a chain, (b) pick a seed-controlled crash height/cursor-skew/coin-skew, (c) replay boot + reducer_frontier_reconcile_light + repair, (d) assert the resulting frontier+cursors equal a DETERMINISTIC RECOMPUTATION from the seeded state (compute expected hstar/coins-cap, not a hand-pinned vector per seed). Fork-based, reset-safe; reuse seed_schema/seed_all_cursors/put_* helpers.

SIGKILL DURING A REORG UNWIND: the kill-9 crash-replay test (test_utxo_apply_crash_replay.c, seed 0x5EED) uses a LINEAR synthetic chain, so its randomized SIGKILL never lands inside utxo_apply_reorg_unwind_if_needed. Extend that one test: swap the linear chain for the L->W fork topology from test_stage_reorg_unwind_parity.c, set UTXO_AUTHOR_STAGE, seed durable state so step_once enters the unwind, keep the fixed-seed SIGKILL, and after each kill assert the resumed coins_kv commitment is byte-identical to a direct build of W. This closes the one real intersection gap (clean-reorg byte-identity AND kill-9 resume each exist separately; their PRODUCT does not). Do NOT build a new lib/sim/reorg_recovery_simulator — it would duplicate the two existing tests.

CONSENSUS-REJECT EVENT COVERAGE: after Wave 1 routes contextual_check_block_header rejects through EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK, add a test asserting each contextual-header reject reason (bad-diffbits, time-too-old, bad-equihash-solution-size, bad-fork-at-checkpoint, bad-version) emits the event with the expected reason string — pins the new observability so it cannot silently regress to stdout.

PARTIAL-BLOCK APPLY / BIP30 self-write recovery: add a deterministic test that pre-populates coins_view at height H, runs connect_block (reindex path) for the block at H, simulates crash by not advancing the tip cursor, resets coins_view to the partially-applied state, and re-runs connect_block to verify the BIP30 self-write tolerance (connect_block.c:261-276) re-accepts the same block. Currently documented in code but not deterministically reproduced.
strengths_to_preserve: Consensus-parity doctrine is real and enforced: E13 lint gate + test_consensus_parity golden values pin bit-for-bit zclassicd compatibility (no versionbits/BIP9/dynamic-PoW can enter the consensus path). Do not weaken.

AR lifecycle enforcement is compile-time + CI-lint real: no raw sqlite3_step in app code; utxo/block/wallet_key all have before/after_save hooks; the progress.kv kernel-store exception is well-documented and bounded.

Single-serial-supervisor-thread model + recursive global progress_store_tx_lock + BEGIN IMMEDIATE gives the reducer strong, simple isolation — it is why the 'reorg races frontier-repair', 'coins co-commit torn write', and 'batch-write double-unlock' findings were all false positives. Preserve the one-thread sweep + one-lock-across-unwind+apply design.

phashBlock realloc-UAF class is already FIXED via Option A (stable per-node hashBlock storage at chainstate.c:678-684) with regression test test_block_map_grow_phashblock; sanctioned lock-free readers are explicitly documented. This is why the two net-p2p pprev/phashBlock UAF findings were false positives.

LOG_FAIL/LOG_ERR/LOG_NULL are return-bearing macros — this correctly invalidated ~5 'function always returns true / falls through after log' findings across wallet, tor, seed_tape, equihash. Keep the macro contract and the gate that mandates them.

Reorg byte-identity is genuinely proven: test_stage_reorg_unwind_parity.c (real stage author, reorged coins_kv SHA3 == direct build of winning branch), test_reorg_projection_parity.c, test_reorg_parity.c, and kill-9 resume via test_utxo_apply_crash_replay.c (seed 0x5EED) already exist — only their PRODUCT (kill during reorg) is missing.

MCP middleware: refcount-based timeout-context ownership, constant-time bearer-token compare, three-layer rate limiting, and the existing test_mcp_fuzz.c (boundary ints / string len / enum mismatch across all 76 tools, wired at test.c:1058) are solid — the 'no fuzz suite' finding was a false positive.

Crypto: SHA-256 self-test (portable-vs-SHA-NI) gates SHA-NI at boot before any thread spawns; Equihash Wagner-collision validation is fully covered by test_chain.c + test_domain_consensus_equihash*.c; constant-time Ed25519 verify audit. The sha_ni lazy-init 'race' is benign (boot-resolved, deterministic CPUID).

Boot recovery: torn-legacy-coins anchor heal is cryptographically gated (coins_kv migration proof, write-once monotonic flag) and runs single-threaded before reducer/p2p start — the alleged TOCTOU has no second actor. Bounded UTXO-wipe row limits prevent repeat of the 1.3M-UTXO loss incident. Preserve the proof-gate and the refuse-unproven-shapes posture.

strengths: Consensus-parity doctrine enforced: CI lint gates (check-consensus-parity, test_consensus_parity) prevent versionbits/BIP9/dynamic PoW parameter introduction. The values are pinned and any drift is caught.

Comprehensive check-block and check-transaction validation gates: 21 checks across context-free and contextual paths, with proper separation between header-only checks and full-block checks (check_pow parameter allows fast-sync bypass).

Robust error-path logging: LOG_FAIL and LOG_RETURN macros are used throughout validation paths, with context (height, txid, reason) logged on every failure. Consensus rejections emit events (EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK).
Defensive allocation: zcl_malloc/zcl_realloc with checked returns throughout connect_block and coins operations. Memory failures are detected and fatal errors propagate.

BIP30 self-write tolerance for crash recovery: The code correctly identifies and tolerates partial-apply remnants at the same height, allowing the validator to recover from kill-9 mid-connect without wedging forever.

ZIP-209 turnstile enforcement with optional-semantics: Shielded value pools are tracked with has_chain_*_value flags mirroring zclassicd boost::optional. The check only enforces when parent chain value is known, avoiding false positives on imported blocks.
Parallel script verification with workpool: Large blocks with 100+ inputs use worker threads instead of sequential verification, with proper pre-allocation to avoid realloc-induced UAF.
Sapling tree sanity check at connection: After Sapling activation, the code verifies hashFinalSaplingRoot is not all-zeros (lines 639-653), catching zero-initialization bugs early.

Comprehensive test coverage: test_consensus_parity, test_check_block_edge, test_consensus.c, and domain consensus tests cover structure, subsidy, equihash, sigops, coins math, and script interpretation.

DEFENSIVE_CODING.md enforcement: Raw sqlite3_step banned in app code (AR lifecycle gates), malloc/calloc/realloc routed through zcl_* wrappers, error returns logged, AR save hooks wired for critical models.

all.claim: Realloc during pointer collection phase creates UAF hazard — script checks array reallocated while check_ptrs references into old base
all.evidence: Lines 535-549: checks/check_ptrs are reallocated inside the collection loop. Previous pointers in check_ptrs[0..num_checks-1] become dangling if the array moves. The comment at line 312-323 acknowledges the 2026-04-22 SIGSEGV from this pattern on block h=3,070,085 with 400 inputs; the current fix pre-scans to allocate exact capacity, but the code still permits realloc at lines 496-505 and 537-549, creating the same window.
all.fix_sketch: Pre-allocate checks[] and check_ptrs[] arrays to exact capacity BEFORE entering the collection loop (already done at lines 324-350 for initial allocation). Remove or guard the realloc branches at lines 494-506 and 535-549 — no resize should occur once collection begins. Validate at startup that pre-scanned cap >= actual num_checks.

all.claim: Missing input detected but error path logs and returns without advancing recovery state — self-heal information set but no state machine transition triggers reimport
all.evidence: Lines 390-404: when coins_view_cache_have_inputs fails, the code logs the missing input (populating state->missing_txid/vout/has_missing_utxo) but then returns REJECT_INVALID at line 408. The process_block_note_utxo_failure() call that would transition the reducer to reimport mode is in a different module (process_block_self_heal.c). This code path detects the symptom but does not kick the recovery machinery.
all.fix_sketch: After logging the missing UTXO, call process_block_note_utxo_failure(ms, datadir, height) to trigger the self-heal flow before returning. Ensure the state machine receives the signal so the node can reimport the missing ancestor. Add a guard condition (if missing_utxo_detected && should_auto_heal) before the recovery kick.

all.claim: Difficulty check uses printf with fflush(stdout) instead of structured logging when nBits mismatch — unobservable in production without stderr redirect
all.evidence: Lines 313-320: bare printf to stdout (not stderr) with no event_emitf or LOG_* macro. The fflush is there but output goes to stdout, not the observability layer. This diagnostic about a bad-diffbits failure is invisible in production unless the caller explicitly redirects stdout.
all.fix_sketch: Replace printf+fflush with event_emitf(EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK, 0, 'reason=bad-diffbits header=0x%08x expected=0x%08x ...') or LOG_FAIL macro that routes through structured logging. Ensure the reason and evidence reach the observability sink, not stdout.

all.claim: Subsidy overflow check does not validate subsidy or fees individually before overflow test — could miss pathological cases
all.evidence: Lines 656-668: get_block_subsidy is called and added to fees; the check `fees > INT64_MAX - subsidy` is correct but does not validate that subsidy is in a valid range BEFORE the addition. If subsidy is corrupted (negative, very large), the overflow test might not catch it or might catch the wrong error.
all.fix_sketch: Add a MoneyRange(subsidy) check BEFORE the overflow test at line 657. Ensure subsidy is positive and in consensus bounds. Return bad-cb-reward-invalid if subsidy fails the range check.

all.claim: ContextualCheckBlock docstring claims 'is_ibd gate' skips contextual_check_transaction but does not document why block finality (check 2) uses header timestamp instead of MTP
all.evidence: Lines 131-150: doc says is_ibd gate skips checks 1 (contextual_check_tx). Lines 413-417 of check_block.c explain the BIP113 issue: 'zclassicd hardcodes nLockTimeFlags=0 here, so LOCKTIME_MEDIAN_TIME_PAST branch is never taken.' But the header doc does not mention this consensus-critical detail (using block.time not MTP for finality cutoff). A reader might think nLockTime is checked against MTP in block connection, which would be wrong.
all.fix_sketch: Update the ContextualCheckBlock docstring (lines 128-156) to explicitly state: 'Check 2 (finality) uses block header timestamp, not MTP, matching zclassicd ConnectBlock behavior. This is consensus-critical: BIP113/LOCKTIME_MEDIAN_TIME_PAST applies ONLY to mempool acceptance (accept_to_mempool), NOT block connection.' Reference the code comment at check_block.c:413.

all.claim: Missing UTXO error path is untested — no test_parallel case covers the self-heal failure recovery codepath when inputs are absent
all.evidence: A search of lib/test/src/ finds test_consensus.c and test_check_block_edge.c but no test explicitly exercises connect_block's missing-input handling and the subsequent call to process_block_note_utxo_failure (if it is made). all.claim: Realloc of txdatas can fail silently — nt may be NULL, but txdatas assignment happens unconditionally, creating dangling pointer dereference
all.evidence: Lines 494-505: zcl_realloc may return NULL on failure. Line 497 assigns result to nt. But if nt is NULL and allocation failed, line 505 assigns `txdatas = nt`, which makes txdatas NULL. Then line 507 dereferences txdatas[num_txdatas] → NULL deref. The check at line 498 only verifies !nt (NULL), but does not prevent the assignment.
all.fix_sketch: After zcl_realloc at line 496-497, check `if (!nt)` before updating txdatas. If realloc fails, do not assign NULL to txdatas. Instead, log the error and jump to cleanup (freeing the old txdatas and returning REJECT_FATAL) without touching the old pointer. The pattern at lines 541-547 is correct (it checks !nc || !np); apply the same pattern here.

all.claim: Equihash crypto scheme lookup may return NULL but is used without null-check in the verify call
all.evidence: Lines 105-110: atomic_load may return NULL scheme. Lines 114-115 check `!scheme || scheme->status == CRYPTO_STATUS_RETIRED` and call LOG_FAIL, but LOG_FAIL returns false, so execution stops. However, line 123 calls `scheme->fn.zk_verify(...)` if scheme is non-NULL and status is not RETIRED. If status == CRYPTO_STATUS_UNREGISTERED but scheme is not NULL, the zk_verify function pointer could be uninitialized. The guard at line 114 checks status but does not check for valid fn.zk_verify.
all.fix_sketch: Add a null-check for scheme->fn.zk_verify after the status check. If fn.zk_verify is NULL or uninitialized, log an error and return false. Alternatively, strengthen the scheme validity check to ensure scheme->fn.zk_verify is callable before line 123.

all.claim: block_undo allocation failure returns via LOG_FAIL but does not free blockundo on the error path before returning
all.evidence: Lines 288-296: block_undo_init called, then block_undo_alloc may fail. Line 292 calls block_undo_free and logs failure. But the blockundo struct itself is stack-allocated (line 288), so the free is correct. However, if alloc fails and we return early, subsequent allocation blocks for checks/check_ptrs (lines 338-350) may also fail, and we free blockundo again at line 348. This double-free is prevented by the immediate return at line 293 (LOG_FAIL returns). The pattern is safe but fragile — if LOG_FAIL were ever changed to not return, this would leak.
all.fix_sketch: This is actually correctly handled (LOG_FAIL guarantees return), but add a comment at line 293: '// LOG_FAIL returns, so blockundo is freed before return and no subsequent code runs'. This documents the invariant for future maintainers.

all.claim: g_last_coins_flush is atomic but never atomically compared with block height — potential race on the flush decision
all.evidence: Line 59: g_last_coins_flush is _Atomic(int64_t). But callers that read it (presumably in flush_coins_if_needed) do so without holding the same lock as the writer, so a stale read is possible. This is not a data race (the read is atomic), but it is a logic race where the decision 'is it time to flush?' can race with concurrent block writes.
all.fix_sketch: This is a benign race — the worst case is an extra flush or a delayed flush, neither of which breaks consensus. If tighter bounds are needed, document that flush_coins_if_needed() is best-effort and may be called more or less frequently than the block_interval suggests. Add a comment: '// benign race: worst case is extra/delayed flush, not data corruption'.

verified.reason: REFUTED as written. The finding misreads the architecture on three independent counts. (1) connect_block() has exactly ONE production caller: the offline -reindex-chainstate tool at config/src/boot_index.c:244 (reindex_chainstate). There a connect_block failure is INTENTIONALLY fatal — boot_index.c:245-250 prints reject_reason and breaks "to prevent UTXO corruption" because "continuing would skip this block's UTXOs." It is correct, not a missed recovery. (2) The live forward-progress path does NOT call connect_block at all (grep over process_block_core/tip_publish/chain_activation_service/app/jobs = empty); missing-input self-heal on the live path is the reducer's already-wired prevout_unresolved machinery in app/jobs/src/script_validate_stage.c + stage_repair_coin_backfill_util.c (tested by test_stage_repair_coin_backfill.c). (3) The proposed fix function process_block_note_utxo_failure() has ZERO production callers — it is reached only from tests; process_block.h:255 even documents it is NOT called from the process_block path. Wiring it into connect_block (i.e. into reindex) would be actively harmful: on >=5 fails it does a tip stage-unwind + reducer_kick (process_block_self_heal.c:79-104), which would corrupt an in-progress reindex replay. The HIGH resilience claim is a false positive. The only genuine (LOW) defect in this region is dead code: state->missing_txid / missing_vout / has_missing_utxo set at connect_block.c:400-402 have zero readers anywhere in the tree, and the line-389 comment "Find the specific missing input for self-healing recovery" describes a flow that no longer exists (the legacy block-connect/disconnect engine was purged — see process_block_self_heal.c:17-20).
verified.refined_fix: Do NOT call process_block_note_utxo_failure() from connect_block — that would damage the reindex path and wire into orphaned code. Instead, either (a) delete the dead self-heal fields (struct uint256 missing_txid; uint32_t missing_vout; bool has_missing_utxo in lib/consensus/include/consensus/validation.h:43-45, their resets at :56-58, and the population at connect_block.c:400-402) since nothing reads them, OR (b) if they are intentionally retained for a future reindex telemetry path, fix the stale comment at connect_block.c:389 to say these fields are diagnostic-only and not currently consumed, and add a one-line note that live missing-input recovery is the reducer prevout_unresolved path (app/jobs/src/script_validate_stage.c), not this function. Prefer (a) subtraction per the project's less-is-more doctrine.

verified.reason: CONFIRMED but severity overstated. The printf+fflush(stdout) at 313-317 is real and is the ONLY printf in check_block.c and accept_block_header.c — an anomaly against the file's own established structured-logging pattern (the dedicated consensus_reject_block_emit() helper at lines 38-54 routes rejections through event_emitf(EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK,...)). The rich bad-diffbits diagnostic (header=0x.., expected=0x.., prev_height/bits) goes to stdout, NOT the observability sink, so it IS invisible in production. HOWEVER the claim "unobservable" is too strong: (1) state->reject_reason is set to "bad-diffbits" and reaches the validation/P2P layer; (2) the caller accept_block_header.c:393 emits a STDERR line via LOG_FAIL ("contextual_check_block_header failed for header at height N"), and stderr is the node.log sink. So the *event* of rejection is logged; only the detailed nBits evidence is lost to stdout. Additionally, contextual_check_block_header() — unlike check_block_header() (line 135) and check_block() (line 186) — never calls consensus_reject_block_emit(), so this whole contextual-header reject path currently emits no EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK event regardless of the printf. This is a genuine observability gap on a consensus-reject path, but it is not a correctness/consensus bug and not fully unobservable, hence MEDIUM not HIGH.
verified.refined_fix: Delete the printf+fflush(stdout) at 313-317. Before the REJECT_IF at 318, fold the rich detail into the reject reason / event. Two options: (A) minimal — replace with event_emitf(EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK, 0, "hash=%s reason=bad-diffbits header=0x%08x expected=0x%08x prev_height=%d prev_bits=0x%08x", hex, header->nBits, expected_bits, pindex_prev->nHeight, pindex_prev->nBits) then REJECT_IF(true,...). (B) cleaner/structural — make contextual_check_block_header() emit on failure like its sibling wrappers: add `if (!ok) consensus_reject_block_emit(header, state);` at the function's failure boundary (requires splitting into _impl like check_block_header/check_block_impl), so ALL contextual-header rejections (bad-equihash-solution-size, bad-diffbits, time-too-old, bad-fork-at-checkpoint, bad-version) gain the EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK event uniformly; carry the nBits detail by appending to state->reject_reason. Prefer (B) — it closes the broader gap that this path emits no consensus-reject event at all. EV_CONSENSUS_REJECT_BLOCK and event_emitf are confirmed present (event.h:185, :285).

all.claim: coins_kv_set_applied_height_in_tx(db, (int32_t)next_cursor) is called AFTER the log row write (line 418) but BEFORE the transaction commit (stage_run_once line 347). However, coins_applied_height is written to progress_meta, not the stage_cursor, so a torn write of coins_applied_height alone (without the cursor advance) can leave coins_applied_height > utxo_apply cursor on restart.

This is correct but conflates two distinct freshness guarantees: (1) coins from block N are durable before block N+1 applies, and (2) coins_kv_get_coins in projection_live_lookup reads coins committed in the current txn (not a cache miss to an older snapshot).

all.fix_sketch: Clarify the comment: 'coins_kv is authored IN this stage's BEGIN IMMEDIATE txn (lines 359-370). projection_live_lookup (line 491) runs inside the same txn, so it sees coins from all PRIOR-COMMITTED blocks plus any coins added earlier in THIS block's apply. Cross-block coin freshness is guaranteed because the prior block's apply committed (and released progress_store_tx_lock) before this block's reorg_unwind (line 630) and apply (line 637) begin.'

verified.reason: The finding's core claim — that a reorg unwind could race a frontier repair / repair_coin_backfill insert "without isolation" — is refuted on two independent grounds. (1) NO CONCURRENCY IS POSSIBLE: both the utxo_apply stage (unwind + step_apply, run via staged_sync_supervisor.c's staged.utxo_apply child) and the frontier-repair condition (reducer_frontier_reconcile_light, run via self_heal.c -> condition_engine_tick) execute as on_tick callbacks on the SINGLE supervisor thread. supervisor.c:248-320 sweep_once() iterates one flat global g_contracts[] array serially, invoking each on_tick one at a time; supervisor.c:323 supervisor_thread_main is the only loop. Domains ('chain','op' in domains.c) are organizational labels — supervisor_register_in_domain (supervisor.c:142) inserts into the same array, there is NO per-domain thread. So unwind and repair cannot execute simultaneously. (2) EVEN IF CROSS-THREAD, ISOLATION HOLDS: every coins_kv mutator already serializes on the global RECURSIVE progress_store_tx_lock (progress_store.c:31-45 recursive-mutex init, :138-147) inside BEGIN IMMEDIATE — the unwind (utxo_apply_stage_step_once holds the lock across both unwind and stage_run_once, lines 628-638), step_apply's apply_coins_kv (under stage.c:289 lock + :316 BEGIN IMMEDIATE), and the named repair_coin_backfill insert (stage_repair_coin_backfill.c:269-271 comment 'the caller's progress_store_tx_lock serializes the window' + :285 BEGIN IMMEDIATE + public entries acquire the lock at :439/:519/:576/:646/:662). The only cross-thread driver (synchronous reducer_ingest_block) is additionally excluded via reducer_drive_active() (staged_sync_supervisor.c:170-177). The unwind+step sharing one lock hold is the INTENDED design (comment at utxo_apply_stage.c:624-627: drain the disconnect in the same critical section before the forward apply), not a hazard. The finding's own fix_sketch concludes the code 'does' maintain isolation and proposes no change.
verified.refined_fix: No code change required — the claimed race cannot occur (single serial supervisor thread) and all coins_kv writers already share one recursive global progress_store_tx_lock + BEGIN IMMEDIATE. If anything is worth doing it is pure documentation (a one-line cross-reference in utxo_apply_stage.c near line 628 noting that step_apply and the frontier-repair condition are serialized by the single supervisor sweep thread, not just the lock), which is SAFE-MECHANICAL and optional — not a HIGH defensive finding.

verified.reason: The finding's core assertion — that "no deterministic simulation validates this invariant under crash scenarios" — is substantially REFUTED. Both halves of the invariant are already deterministically simulated. (1) Byte-identity under a clean reorg: test_stage_reorg_unwind_parity.c is the keystone proof — it drives the REAL stage author (UTXO_AUTHOR_STAGE / utxo_apply_reorg_unwind_if_needed), builds losing branch L, installs heavier winning branch W, and asserts the reorged coins_kv SHA3 commitment is BYTE-IDENTICAL to a direct build of W (its header at lines 1-40 spells this out: "A reorg on that path must produce a BYTE-IDENTICAL coins_kv SHA3 commitment to a direct build of the winning branch"). test_reorg_projection_parity.c and test_reorg_parity.c (937 lines) prove the same for the projection and legacy coins.db paths. (2) Crash/kill-9 mid-commit recovery: test_utxo_apply_crash_replay.c SIGKILLs the REAL stage co-commit mid-BEGIN-IMMEDIATE (fixed seed 0x5EED, reproducible offsets per its header lines 20-23), reopens the store raw (WAL recovery = next boot), resumes from the durable cursor, and asserts coins content == recomputed expectation after EVERY kill — exactly the build→kill→resume→verify pattern the fix sketch requests. The atomicity that makes crash-mid-reorg safe is also explicit and load-bearing: utxo_apply_stage.c:628-636 runs the unwind inside ONE progress_store_tx_lock txn and on failure leaves the cursor untouched ("the next tick retries"), so a crash mid-unwind reduces by construction to the torn-txn-rolled-back-by-WAL case crash_replay already exercises. The doc text at 153-157 is ACCURATE (not stale/wrong), so the cited DOC location is a misattribution. The one genuine residual: the crash-replay test uses a LINEAR synthetic chain (test_utxo_apply_crash_replay.c:119, contiguous heights), so its SIGKILL never lands DURING a reorg unwind; and the reorg-parity test does a clean reorg with no kill. The exact product scenario (SIGKILL mid-utxo_apply_reorg_unwind_if_needed → resume → assert byte-identity) is not covered by any single test. That is a narrow, defensible test-coverage delta, not an unvalidated invariant — hence LOW, and confirmed=false on the finding as written.
verified.refined_fix: Do NOT build the large new lib/sim/reorg_recovery_simulator the finding proposes — it would duplicate test_stage_reorg_unwind_parity.c (clean-reorg byte-identity) and test_utxo_apply_crash_replay.c (kill-9 resume byte-identity) that already exist. The minimal, honest fix is to close the one real intersection gap by extending test_utxo_apply_crash_replay.c: replace its linear synthetic chain with the L→W fork topology from test_stage_reorg_unwind_parity.c, set UTXO_AUTHOR_STAGE, and seed the durable state so that step_once enters utxo_apply_reorg_unwind_if_needed; keep the existing fixed-seed (0x5EED) randomized SIGKILL so a kill can land mid-unwind; after each kill, reopen and assert the resumed coins_kv commitment is byte-identical to a direct build of W (reuse its commitment helper). This is one test edit, fork-based, reset-safe, deterministic, and ungated — it adds the missing product case without a new simulator. Separately, a one-line doc improvement: at FRAMEWORK.md:157 append a pointer to the three parity tests + the crash-replay test so the byte-identity claim cites its proofs.

verified.reason: The finding's two load-bearing claims are both wrong, so HIGH is unwarranted. (1) GATE claim is FALSE: tools/lint/check_no_raw_clock_outside_platform.sh matches only `clock_gettime(`, `time(NULL)`, `getrandom(` via its regex (line 19) — it does NOT match `gettimeofday(`. The gate is green with this code today, and there are 24 raw gettimeofday sites repo-wide (e.g. sibling lib/net/src/msgprocessor.c:580), so this site is not singled out and not a ratchet violation. (2) LAW 4 claim is MISAPPLIED: FRAMEWORK.md:81 Law 4 says "No clock, no RNG, no I/O in domain/" — it is scoped to the pure core (domain/), and the same line states "the shell is dirty." snapshot_sync_service.c is a SERVICE shape under app/services/ (the shell), not domain/ and not a projection; the lint enforcement is the projections-are-pure gate (FRAMEWORK.md:304) which binds only projection files. A clock in a service does not violate Law 4. (3) DETERMINISTIC-REPLAY claim is overstated: snapsync_now_us_internal feeds ONLY RAM-only operational timers — blacklist expiry, stall/negotiation/receive timeouts, and elapsed-time logging (lines 148,165,395,401,436,470,603 + fetch/verify/offer callers). grep confirms none of start_time_us/last_progress_time_us/blacklisted_at_us are persisted to the reducer/projection/log, so this is NOT on the deterministic-consensus replay path; nothing replayable is corrupted. The one residual kernel of truth (LOW): platform_time_monotonic_us() routes through the injectable clock_now_* source (sim/seed_tape override it) AND is monotonic, whereas gettimeofday is wall-clock and can jump backward under NTP, briefly skewing a stall timer. So the swap is a minor robustness/consistency improvement, not a HIGH resilience defect.
verified.refined_fix: Optional LOW-value consistency cleanup: replace the gettimeofday body of snapsync_now_us_internal() with `return platform_time_monotonic_us();` (#include "platform/time_compat.h"). Use the MONOTONIC variant only — these call sites are all elapsed-time deltas (now - start_time_us / now - last_progress_time_us / now - blacklisted_at_us), so monotonic is strictly more correct (immune to wall-clock jumps) and avoids the stall-timer skew gettimeofday can produce. Do NOT use the wall-time variant the finding suggests: `platform_time_wall_time_us()` does not exist in time_compat.h (only platform_time_wall_unix / platform_time_wall_time_t), and wall-time would reintroduce the backward-jump bug. Because this is a repo-wide pattern (24 sites), treat it as a sweep candidate, not a snapshot-specific bug; if swept, the existing snapshot stall-condition tests (lib/test/src/test_snapshot_*_condition.c, which already call snapsync_now_us_internal directly) gain the ability to be driven by an injected clock for deterministic simulation — that is the only concrete upside.

verified.location: lib/net/include/net/net.h:227 (compact_pending_block); dereference sites lib/net/src/msg_compact.c:309-344; free sites lib/net/src/net.c:387-390 and lib/net/src/msg_compact.c:28-31
verified.reason: The claimed UAF requires disconnect/free to run on a different thread concurrently with compact-block reconstruction. That race does not exist. Message processing is single-threaded per peer: one thread_message_handler (connman.c:1478-1488) drives connman_run_message_cycle, which calls msg_process_messages (msgprocessor.c:1144), which drains a peer's recv queue SERIALLY in a while-loop on one thread — cmpctblock and blocktxn for the same peer are never dispatched concurrently. compact_pending_block is freed in only two places: (1) p2p_node_free (net.c:387) and (2) compact_pending_clear (msg_compact.c:28). (1) cannot run during a message cycle because p2p_node_free is gated on ref_count: the message cycle bumps ref_count under cs_nodes before releasing the lock (connman.c:1400), and BOTH free paths defer when ref_count>0 (connman.c:1318-1321 socket sweep; connman.c:1470-1473 deferred_free_sweep). The codebase documents this exact machinery (connman.c:1311-1358, 1380-1384, 1459-1476) as the reason a peer 'cannot be freed out from under us'. (2) is called synchronously from within process_blocktxn AFTER the dereference, on the same thread. Furthermore, process_blocktxn already null-checks: 'if (!node->compact_pending_block || memcmp(...hash...))' at msg_compact.c:309-311 before any use, so even a cleared pointer is handled. The proposed fix (atomic_exchange on disconnect, generation counter) guards a cross-thread window the dispatch model precludes. The finding misreads the concurrency model.
verified.refined_fix: No code change needed; the invariant is already enforced by the refcount/deferred-free model. If anything is warranted it is a DOC-only improvement: add a one-line comment at net.h:227 stating 'compact_pending_block is owned by the single message-handler thread; freed only by p2p_node_free (ref_count-gated) or compact_pending_clear (synchronous, same thread). No cross-thread access — no atomics required.' That is SAFE-MECHANICAL and purely clarifying, not a fix for a real defect.

verified.reason: The TOCTOU premise has no second actor. urs_coins_kv_is_proven_authority() runs synchronously from boot.c:1706, the coins-view-open gate during single-threaded boot setup. The only thread alive before this gate is load_params_thread (boot.c:968), which loads zk-SNARK verification keys (sapling_init_params) and never touches progress.kv or any of the three keys. ALL writers of coins_kv_migration_complete / coins_kv_count / coins_applied_height run AFTER this gate: the reducer Jobs/sync services start at reducer_activation (boot.c:3354) and p2p_services_start (boot.c:3566); coins_kv_boot_rebuild_if_needed is also called synchronously later in boot (after line 1721). So between the three reads there is no concurrent writer that could "mutate the proof." Additionally, the finding's specific scenario — "migration flag cleared mid-check" — is impossible by construction: coins_kv_migration_complete is monotonic, set to 1 in four places (coins_kv_boot_rebuild.c:48/94/135/167) and never reset to 0 or deleted in any production path (grep for delete/clear of the key returns nothing). The claim misreads a one-shot single-threaded boot decision as a concurrent path. The accept/refuse logic of the proof gate is also already covered by lib/test/src/test_boot_coins_anchor_dual_store_recovery.c (e.g. RED-2: migration_complete UNSET -> refuses -> FATAL). The residual D5 sentiment (this recovery deserves simulation) is partly fair as a general observation, but the named defect — a TOCTOU race requiring fork/kill-9 mid-proof-check simulation — is not real.

verified.reason: REFUTED. The finding's central evidence — "the function ALWAYS returns true at line 918, even when the UPDATE fails" — is factually wrong. LOG_FAIL is defined at lib/util/include/util/log_macros.h:29-33 as `do { fprintf(...); return false; } while (0)` — a return-false macro, NOT a log-and-continue. At line 909-917, when the next_child UPDATE step fails (step_rc != SQLITE_DONE), the body calls LOG_FAIL(...), which returns false at line 916. Execution never reaches the `return true;` at line 918 on the UPDATE-failure path. Likewise the INSERT-failure path (line 879-881) and the prepare/bind failures (895-906) all return false via LOG_FAIL. Line 918 is reached only on full success. The finding describes the PRE-FIX code: the comment at lines 883-890 explicitly narrates that "The old code ignored every sqlite rc on this UPDATE... Now every op has its rc observed and any failure propagates up," and the inner comment (910-913) says "treat it as a hard failure so the caller ... rollback the whole transaction." The fix_sketch ("Change line 918 to `return ok;`") targets a version that no longer exists. Atomicity is also already enforced: the caller wallet_sqlite_flush_r wraps the flush in BEGIN/COMMIT and on any write_sapling_key→false does `goto rollback`→ROLLBACK (lines 1169-1179, 1215-1227), so a false return discards the INSERT too — no torn state, no duplicate child index, no unlinkability loss. The described bug cannot occur in the current code.
verified.refined_fix: No code change required — the alleged bug does not exist in the current code. The reviewer mistook the return-false LOG_FAIL macro for a non-returning log. Recommend the reviewer's tooling resolve macros (LOG_FAIL/LOG_ERR/LOG_NULL all `return`) before flagging "function always returns X". Optional hardening unrelated to this finding: a regression test (test_parallel fork-based) that injects an UPDATE-step failure and asserts write_sapling_key returns false AND that flush rolls back the INSERT, to pin the now-correct behavior against future regressions.

It is registered at test.c:1058. The finding appears to have looked only at tools/mcp/ (the controllers/router source) without checking lib/test/src/ (where the tests live). If any incremental hardening were desired, it would be marginal: assert on the SPECIFIC error code returned by mcp_router_validate (ENUM_MISMATCH, STRING_TOO_LONG, INT_OUT_OF_RANGE) rather than only no-crash/error-envelope for the boundary cases — but that is a minor strengthening, not the missing-coverage gap the finding describes.

verified.reason: The finding is refuted by its own evidence. (1) The cited path/symbol is wrong: there is no `mcp_server.c` with a `handle_tools_call` — the real caller is `tools/mcp_server.c:handle_tools_call`, and the dispatch call is at line 110-111, with the guard at 112. (2) The claim's core assertion — "does not null-check result before strdup" — is false. Line 112 IS the null-check: `if (!result) result = strdup("null");`. There is no `strdup(result)` anywhere; `result` is first dereferenced at line 115 (`strlen(result)`), which only runs after line 112 guarantees non-NULL. So even when dispatch returns NULL, no NULL is ever passed to strdup and no NULL is dereferenced. (3) Reachability of a NULL return is itself near-nil: every error path in mcp_router_dispatch returns `out ? out : strdup("{\"error\":...}")` (router.c:531,552,574), the success path is guarded by `rc != 0 || res.body == NULL` (router.c:560), and mcp_middleware_dispatch's error returns go through mcp_router_error_envelope_strdup which returns NULL only at router.c:482 under zcl_malloc OOM. The single OOM path that can yield NULL is exactly the one the caller's line-112 guard handles. No crash is reachable. Severity HIGH is unjustified — there is no null-deref and no unguarded strdup.
verified.refined_fix: No fix required for correctness — the caller already handles NULL safely at tools/mcp_server.c:112. Optionally, as pure defense-in-depth (not a bug fix), make mcp_router_error_envelope_strdup at router.c:482 return the static OOM fallback instead of NULL: replace `if (!out) return NULL;` with `if (!out) return strdup("{\"error\":{\"code\":-32603,\"message\":\"internal\"}}");` — mirroring the `out ? out : strdup(...)` idiom already used at router.c:531/552/574 so mcp_middleware_dispatch becomes total. This is cosmetic robustness, not a fix for any reachable defect, and should be filed as LOW at most.

verified.location: lib/crypto/src/sha256.c:393-395 (lazy init in sha256_transform); detection in detect_sha_ni() lib/crypto/src/sha256.c:34-72; boot resolution at config/src/boot.c:924 (sha256_selftest) preceding the only candidate thread spawn at config/src/boot.c:968
verified.reason:
The claimed HIGH concurrency race is not reachable in the production path, so the finding is a false positive as stated; I refute it on three independent grounds.

1) The variable is fully resolved at boot BEFORE any thread is spawned. boot_step_init_crypto_and_state() (config/src/boot.c:910-977) calls sha256_selftest() at line 924, which calls detect_sha_ni() (sha256.c:408). The ONLY pthread_create in that whole boot step — the load_params_thread spawn at line 968 — runs AFTER line 924, inside the same function. boot_step_init_crypto_and_state itself runs at app_init line 1221, before any block-index load, chain work, or service/net startup. So by the time any thread that could call sha256_transform exists, sha_ni_available is already 0 or 1.

2) detect_sha_ni() never leaves the sentinel: every return path assigns sha_ni_available=0 (lines 38,43,66,70) or =1 (line 64). After the first call it is permanently 0/1, so the `< 0` branch at sha256.c:393 is never re-entered concurrently in steady state.

3) Even the theoretical "two threads both observe -1 on the very first call" is a BENIGN data race: CPUID is deterministic, so every racing writer computes the IDENTICAL value; a torn int read still converges to the same 0/1. Worst case is redundant CPUID work + redundant self-test, never a wrong dispatch. (And pthread_create at line 968 establishes a happens-before edge making the boot write visible to spawned threads on x86, the only arch this code compiles on — guarded by __x86_64__||__i386__ + __SHA__.)

The finding's fix sketch (pthread_once / constructor / atomics) misreads the design: the lazy init at 393-395 is a belt-and-suspenders fallback, not the real init site — the real init is the boot self-test. The claim does not cite that the boot path already serializes detection before threading, which is the dispositive fact it overlooks. HIGH is wrong; there is no real concurrency hazard. I downgrade to LOW for the residual point that the lazy-init is technically an unsynchronized check-then-act and nothing enforces the boot ordering at the type level — worth a cheap hardening, not a HIGH bug.
verified.refined_fix: Optional, defensive-only (the production race is not reachable). Change `static int sha_ni_available = -1;` to `static _Atomic(int) sha_ni_available = -1;` and access it with atomic_load_explicit(..., memory_order_relaxed) / atomic_store_explicit(..., memory_order_relaxed) at the read sites (sha256.c:393,395,408,409,449,450) and write sites (38,43,64,66,70,435). On x86 an aligned int load/store is already atomic, so this is zero runtime cost and merely (a) makes the benign first-call race well-defined under C11, and (b) silences TSan/UBSan if the lazy-init path is ever exercised in a test before boot. Do NOT add pthread_once or a constructor — that would over-engineer a fallback path and could reorder the self-test (sha256_selftest at boot.c:924) that intentionally gates SHA-NI on a portable-vs-hardware byte match. Separately, a 1-line comment at sha256.c:392 documenting "sha_ni_available is resolved by sha256_selftest() at boot before any thread spawns; this lazy init is a fallback for non-boot callers" would record the load-bearing ordering invariant (D6).

verified.reason: The finding's mechanism is a misread and its severity is unjustified. FACTUAL kernel that is true: equihash_is_valid_solution (lib/crypto/src/equihash.c:311) has no NULL-guard on p/base_state/soln, and line 315 evaluates `soln_len != p->solution_width` which dereferences p. But the finding's specific framing — "crash before LOG_FAIL executes" — misreads the control flow: line 315 is an `if` CONDITION and LOG_FAIL (a log-and-return-false macro, log_macros.h:29) is its BODY; LOG_FAIL ordering is irrelevant. The real question is reachability of p==NULL, and it is NOT reachable. Every production caller passes the address of a stack-local struct: domain/consensus/src/equihash.c:60,90 (`struct equihash_params ep; ... equihash_is_valid_solution(&ep, &state, ...)`) and lib/crypto_registry/src/scheme_equihash_200_9.c:30,37 (same `&ep`/`&state` pattern, with soln itself null-checked at line 22). All ~7 test callers likewise pass `&ep`/`&state`. It is structurally impossible for p, base_state, or soln to be NULL at any call site. This is a lib/ crypto primitive, not app-layer code, so the AR/MCP defensive gates do not apply; the function's contract is already documented in crypto/equihash.h:58-70. There is no reachable crash, so HIGH is wrong and the bug-as-described does not exist.
verified.refined_fix: No correctness fix required. If pure defensive hardening is still desired for this consensus-path primitive, the minimal SAFE-MECHANICAL change is a documented precondition rather than the finding's branch logic: add a one-line contract comment plus a debug-only assert at function entry, e.g. `/* requires: p, base_state, soln all non-NULL; all callers pass &ep/&state (see domain/consensus/equihash.c, crypto_registry/scheme_equihash_200_9.c). */` and `assert(p && base_state && soln);`. Do NOT add a runtime `if(!p) LOG_FAIL(...)` returning false on the consensus verifier path — silently returning false for an internal NULL would mask a programming bug as a PoW-invalid block rather than failing loudly, which is the wrong failure mode for a consensus check. Prefer the assert (loud in debug, free in release) over a soft-fail. This is hardening, not a fix for any reachable defect.

verified.reason: REFUTED as a CRITICAL defect. The claim that "caller cannot detect allocation failure" is false. eh_row_from_hash unconditionally assigns `row->data = zcl_calloc(1, width, ...)` at line 236 BEFORE any early return, so on failure `row->data` holds the NULL that calloc returned. On NULL it returns at line 237 immediately, before any of lines 238-240 dereference data — so there is no NULL-deref inside the function. Every one of the three call sites (batch8 line 372, batch4 line 396, scalar line 411) performs `if (!X[i+k].data)` right after the call and, on failure, frees all prior rows + X + indices + tmp_hash and exits via LOG_FAIL (which expands to `return false` per log_macros.h:32) with full context. So allocation failure IS reliably detectable and IS detected at every caller, with correct cleanup and logging — no use-before-check (the finding's own evidence concedes "The NULL check at line 372 catches this"), no leak, no silent continuation, no consensus impact (a calloc failure correctly rejects the PoW with a logged error). The finding self-contradicts: it labels CRITICAL while admitting the downstream check works. At most this is a minor style preference (void out-param + post-check vs. bool return); it is not a defensive-gate violation and nowhere near CRITICAL.
verified.refined_fix: Optional readability-only nit, NOT required for correctness: convert eh_row_from_hash to `static bool eh_row_from_hash(...)` returning `false` on calloc failure (and `true` otherwise), then have the three callers check the return value instead of `!X[i+k].data`. This makes the failure contract explicit at the signature level. Purely cosmetic — current behavior is already correct and fully cleaned up. If left as-is, no action needed; the post-call NULL checks are the established, working contract.

verified.reason:
The finding's load-bearing evidence — "No grep of lib/test/ finds 'test_equihash' ... indicating unit test coverage" — is a false negative from searching the wrong identifier. The consensus verifier equihash_is_valid_solution IS directly unit-tested in at least 5 locations across 3 registered test functions, and the existing vectors already cover everything the finding proposes:
(1) known-valid Equihash-200/9-class solution + header: the canonical (96,5) Zcash reference witness kValidIndices_96_5 is verified to return TRUE at test_chain.c:667, test_domain_consensus_equihash.c:248, and test_domain_consensus_equihash_seal.c:371.
(2) invalid solution (final XOR non-zero / wrong index): perturbed index 2261->2262 at test_chain.c:692-708 must return false; bad[0]^=1 at test_domain_consensus_equihash_seal.c:376-383 must return false; all-zero solution across ALL 4 recognized sizes (36/68/400/1344) at test_domain_consensus_equihash.c:132-149 must return valid=false.
(3) non-colliding-rows (equihash.c:437) and out-of-order-indices (equihash.c:444) branches are exercised by the perturbed-index negatives, which break the Wagner collision tree.
All three test entry points are registered in test_helpers.h and invoked at test.c:656,810,1030, so they run under test_parallel. The seal test explicitly pairs a true-positive accept with a negative reject precisely to guard the 'false-reject-everything' failure mode the finding worried about. The verifier is well-covered; the claim of zero coverage misreads the codebase.
verified.refined_fix: No new test file is needed; the proposed coverage already exists. If any incremental hardening is wanted (optional, low value), it would be to add an explicit dedicated negative that forces the out-of-order-indices branch (equihash.c:444) by swapping a colliding pair's order in a known-valid witness, and one that forces the non-distinct-indices branch (equihash.c:451) by duplicating an index — these two specific internal reject branches are currently only hit incidentally via perturbation rather than by a targeted vector. That is a SAFE-MECHANICAL test-only addition, not a gap that justifies a HIGH finding.

verified.reason: The core claim — that the cited path is "unsafe" / leaks because db can be non-NULL on failure — is REFUTED. The code at 498-500 already closes db on the non-NULL path: `if (db) sqlite3_close(db); return 0;`. SQLite's documented contract: sqlite3_open_v2 writes a non-NULL handle into *db on every failure EXCEPT SQLITE_NOMEM (where *db is set to NULL). db is initialized to NULL at line 497, so: on SQLITE_NOMEM `if(db)` correctly skips (nothing allocated); on any other error `if(db)` correctly closes. Also sqlite3_close(NULL) is itself a documented no-op, so `if (db) sqlite3_close(db)` is functionally identical to the unconditional close the fix_sketch proposes — there is no leak and no behavior difference to gain. The finding ironically cites the WELL-HANDLED location: line 108-109 (`if (sqlite3_open_v2(...) != SQLITE_OK) return;`) is the one with an actual missing close, not 498-500. The only genuine residual at 498-500 is the secondary point: the failure returns silently with no LOG_FAIL (util/log_macros.h is already #included at line 10), a minor defensive-logging gap, not a HIGH resource-safety bug. Severity adjusted HIGH->LOW accordingly.
verified.refined_fix: No resource fix is needed at 498-500 — the close is already correct. The only worthwhile change is observability: add `LOG_FAIL("onion: open node.db RO failed: %s", sqlite3_errmsg(db));` before `return 0;` (db is valid to query for errmsg on the non-NULL failure path; guard or fetch the message before close). Separately, if the real intent was to fix an open-failure leak, the actual target is line 108-109 (`get_self_stats` path) which does NOT close db on failure — change it to mirror the 498-500 pattern (`if (db) sqlite3_close(db); return;`).
